stem: Find the particular solution \( y_p = A e^{[[k]]x} \) of \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = [[M]]\,e^{[[k]]x} \). What is the coefficient \( A \)?
solution: Substituting \( y_p = A e^{[[k]]x} \) gives \( A([[k]]^{2} + ([[b]])[[k]] + ([[c]]))e^{[[k]]x} = [[M]]\,e^{[[k]]x} \). Since \( [[k]] \) is not a root of the characteristic equation, \( A = \frac{[[M]]}{[[k]]^{2} + ([[b]])[[k]] + ([[c]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the \( ([[b]])[[k]] \) term from the denominator.
This uses only the constant coefficient \( [[c]] \) in the denominator, dropping the derivative terms.
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by two from the correct coefficient.
This is off by two from the correct coefficient.

stem: Find the particular solution \( y_p = A \) (a constant) of \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = [[M]] \). What is \( A \)?
solution: Since \( y_p = A \) is constant, \( y_p' = y_p'' = 0 \), so the equation reduces to \( ([[c]])A = [[M]] \), giving \( A = \dfrac{[[M]]}{[[c]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by the coefficient of \( y' \) instead of the coefficient of \( y \), which cannot appear since a constant has zero derivatives.
This has the wrong sign.
This multiplies instead of dividing.
This forgets to divide by \( c \) at all.
This divides by \( b+c \) instead of \( c \) alone.
This is one more than the correct coefficient.
This is one less than the correct coefficient.

stem: Find the particular solution \( y_p = A\cos([[k]]x) + B\sin([[k]]x) \) of \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = [[M]]\cos([[k]]x) \). What is the coefficient \( A \)?
solution: Substituting \( y_p \) and matching the coefficients of \( \cos([[k]]x) \) and \( \sin([[k]]x) \) gives the system \( ([[c]]-[[k]]^{2})A + ([[b]])[[k]]B = [[M]] \) and \( -([[b]])[[k]]A + ([[c]]-[[k]]^{2})B = 0 \). Solving this linear system for \( A \) gives \( A = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the coupling from the \( y' \) term and solves as if \( B \) were zero.
This divides by the \( y' \)-coupling term alone, ignoring the \( (c-k^2) \) term.
This has the wrong sign, missing the coupling from \( B \).
This multiplies instead of dividing by \( (c-k^2) \).
This divides by \( c \) alone instead of \( c - k^2 \).
This is one more than the correct coefficient.
This is one less than the correct coefficient.

stem: Find the particular solution \( y_p = Ax + B \) of \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = [[M]]x \). What is the constant term \( B \)?
solution: Substituting \( y_p = Ax + B \) gives \( y_p'' = 0 \) and \( y_p' = A \), so matching coefficients of \( x \) gives \( ([[c]])A = [[M]] \), hence \( A = [[M]]/([[c]]) \); matching constants gives \( ([[b]])A + ([[c]])B = 0 \), so \( B = -\dfrac{([[b]])A}{[[c]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the leading coefficient \( A \) instead of the constant term \( B \).
This flips the sign of \( A \) instead of solving for \( B \).
This forgets to divide by \( c \) a second time when solving for \( B \).
This has the wrong sign in front of the correct expression for \( B \).
This uses \( b \) in the denominator instead of \( c \).
This is one more than the correct constant term.
This is one less than the correct constant term.
